title: 3 places where your CNY can become USD

scripts.subtitle: Classic PAS opener
scripts.original_vo: Listen. If you're a business owner sending CNY to your Chinese supplier, there are three places your CNY can quietly turn into USD before it ever lands. Place one. Your sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront because USD is cheaper for them to route. Place two. The correspondent bank in the middle holds only USD, so it converts again. Place three. Your supplier's receiving bank gets USD and converts back to CNY at their bank's rate. That's three conversion points. Three spreads. Three margins your bank never tells you about. Now your supplier is short. They're calling you asking why they got 47,000 dollars when you sent the equivalent of 50,000. Stop. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. Zero double conversion. Same-day. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

Now your supplier is short. They're calling you asking why they got 47,000 dollars when you sent the equivalent of 50,000. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. Zero double conversion. Same-day. Search WorldFirst.
scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: If you are a business owner in Malaysia or anywhere in Southeast Asia, and you pay suppliers in China in CNY, the Chinese currency, you need to understand something. Your CNY can quietly turn into USD on the way over. Not once. Up to three times. Here is how. Place one. Your own bank, the one sending the money. Most banks do not carry CNY on their pipes. They carry USD. So they swap your CNY into USD before sending. That is conversion number one. Place two. The bank in the middle. This is called a correspondent bank. It sits between your bank and your supplier's bank in China. It usually only holds USD. So if any CNY came through, it gets swapped again here. That is conversion number two. Place three. Your supplier's bank in China. It receives USD. It then swaps that USD back into CNY at its own rate, not yours. That is conversion number three. Three conversions. Three exchange rate spreads. Each one takes a small slice. Your bank never tells you about these slices. So your supplier ends up short. They call you and say, we only got 47,000 dollars when you sent 50,000. Now you have to wire the difference, or eat the loss. We built WorldFirst differently. We send CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. One rate. No double conversion. Same-day delivery in most cases. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Cold open + compounding math
scripts.original_vo: Watch this. You hit send on a CNY payment to your factory. By the time it lands, your CNY has been converted three different times. Once. Twice. Three times. Each conversion takes a 1% spread. Compound that. On a 50,000 dollar order, you just lost 1,500 dollars to invisible currency hops you didn't know existed. Do it twice a month. That's 36,000 a year. Over five years, your bank quietly took 180,000 from your business through conversion points you never saw. That's not a fee. That's the spread on three separate exchange rate hops. Sender bank. Correspondent. Beneficiary bank. Each one takes a bite. Listen. WorldFirst sends CNY direct. No intermediary conversion. No double conversion at the supplier's end. Same-day to Chinese accounts. Your supplier gets exactly what you sent. Period. Search WorldFirst. Period.

But that is not what happens. By the time it lands, your CNY may have been swapped between currencies three different times. Here is why. Each bank in the chain has its own currency rate, called an exchange rate. The gap between what you get on the news and what the bank actually gives you is called the spread. It is a hidden charge baked into the rate. Now picture three banks, each taking around 1% in spread. One percent at your bank. One percent at the bank in the middle, the correspondent bank. One percent at your supplier's bank when it converts back to CNY. On a 50,000 dollar order, that is roughly 1,500 dollars gone. Not as a fee on your statement. As a quiet bite out of the rate. Do this twice a month. That is around 36,000 dollars a year. Stretch it over five years. That is roughly 180,000 dollars your business never sees. That is a hire. A container of stock. A holiday for your family. Those are illustrative numbers based on common bank spreads, but the mechanism is real and happens on most CNY wires. We built WorldFirst differently. We send CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. No bank in the middle. No double conversion at the supplier's end. Same-day in most cases. Your supplier gets exactly what you sent. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: I'll say it opinion-as-truth + compound math
scripts.original_vo: I'll say it. There's no such thing as a CNY wire from most banks. You think you're sending CNY. You're sending USD that gets converted to CNY somewhere down the line. And there are three places that can happen. Your sender bank. The correspondent. The supplier's bank. Each conversion point. Each spread. 1% here, 1% there, 0.5% at the end. That's 2.5% on every payment. On 50,000 dollars that's 1,250 gone. Per wire. Multiply that across a year. 30,000 dollars vanished into currency conversion you didn't know was happening. That's a hire. That's a container. That's your margin. WorldFirst carries CNY natively. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. One conversion. Sometimes zero if you're holding CNY in the account already. The difference goes back into your business. Where it belongs. Period.

scripts.original_vo: Stop using your bank to send CNY. I'm serious. Your bank doesn't carry CNY rails. It carries USD rails. So when you ask it to send CNY, it doesn't send CNY. It sends USD and hopes a conversion happens somewhere down the line. Three somewheres, actually. Sender side conversion. Correspondent bank conversion. Beneficiary bank conversion. Each one a spread. Each one a cost. This is the wrong tool. You wouldn't use a fax machine to send a video. So stop using a USD-rail bank to send CNY. WorldFirst is built on CNY-direct rails. Same-day to Chinese bank accounts. Your supplier receives CNY. Not USD. Not double-converted. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst. Now.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Your bank may not be the right tool for sending CNY to China. Here is why. Banks move international money on what they call rails, the pipes that carry currency between countries. Most banks have rails built for USD. They do not have direct rails for CNY. So when you ask your bank to send CNY, it does not actually send CNY end to end. It sends USD and hopes a swap into CNY happens somewhere down the line. There are three somewheres where that swap can happen. Place one. The sender bank, at the start. Place two. The correspondent bank in the middle, the one that routes the wire onwards. Place three. The beneficiary bank, your supplier's bank in China. Each swap takes a spread, the hidden margin inside the exchange rate. Each spread is a cost to you. Think of it like this. You would not use a fax machine to send a video file. Wrong tool. The pipe does not carry that kind of data. Same idea here. A USD-rail bank is not the right tool for a CNY job. We built WorldFirst on CNY-direct rails into Chinese bank accounts. Same-day in most cases. Your supplier receives CNY. Not USD. Not double converted. Use the right tool for the job. Your margin depends on it. Search WorldFirst.

scripts.subtitle: Truth nobody's telling you / conspiratorial
scripts.original_vo: Here's the truth nobody's telling you about international wires to China. The conversion doesn't happen where you think it happens. You assume your bank converts CNY at the front and sends CNY across. Wrong. Three different banks can convert your money at three different points. Sender bank. Correspondent bank in the middle. Beneficiary bank at the end. Each one books its own rate. Each one takes its own spread. You'll never see this on a statement. You'll just notice your supplier got less than expected. Or worse, your supplier received USD and had to convert again at their own bank. Now there's a fourth conversion. Four spreads on one wire. This is how it actually works. Every single time. WorldFirst doesn't play this game. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same-day. One rate. Period.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: Here is something most people will not tell you about international wires to China. The currency swap does not happen where you think it happens. You probably assume your bank swaps your money into CNY at the front and then sends real CNY across the border. That is not how it actually works for most banks. Three different banks can each swap your money along the way. Place one. Your sender bank, where the money leaves. Place two. The correspondent bank in the middle, the bank that routes the wire from country to country. Place three. The beneficiary bank, your supplier's bank in China. Each one books its own exchange rate. Each one takes its own spread, the hidden gap inside the rate. You will not see these spreads as line items on your statement. You will just notice your supplier got less CNY than you thought you sent. Or worse. Your supplier's account receives USD because the receiving bank could not auto-convert. So your supplier then has to swap USD into CNY themselves at their bank. That is a fourth swap. Four spreads on one wire. We built WorldFirst differently. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. One rate at the start. Same-day delivery in most cases. Search WorldFirst.
scripts.diff_markup: Here's <del>the truth</del><ins>something</ins> nobody's telling you about international wires to China. The conversion doesn't happen where you think it happens. You assume your bank converts CNY at the front and sends CNY across. Wrong. Three different banks can convert your money at three different points. Sender bank. Correspondent bank in the middle. Beneficiary bank at the end. Each one books its own rate. Each one takes its own spread. You'll never see this on a statement. You'll just notice your supplier got less than expected. Or worse, your supplier received USD and had to convert again at their own bank. Now there's a fourth conversion. Four spreads on one wire. This is how it actually works. Every single time. WorldFirst <del>doesn't play this game</del><ins>is built differently</ins>. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts. Same-day. One rate<del>. Period</del>.

scripts.subtitle: Real talk + escalating personal energy
scripts.original_vo: Real talk for every business owner paying suppliers in China. You sent CNY last week. Your supplier called and said they got USD. You're confused. You called your bank. They said it was the correspondent. You called the correspondent. They said it was the receiving bank. The receiving bank said the sender bank should have sent CNY. Nobody can tell you where the conversion happened. Because it could have happened at any of three places. Sender bank converts CNY to USD upfront. Or correspondent never had CNY. Or receiving bank converted on arrival. Three possible conversion points. Three spreads. Zero transparency. I see this every single week. Stop guessing where your money got converted. WorldFirst sends CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. Same-day. One rate. No phone-tag with three different banks. Search WorldFirst. Now.

scripts.iterated_vo_clarity: For every business owner paying suppliers in China. Picture this. You sent CNY last week. Your supplier in China calls you and says, we received USD, not CNY. You are confused. You called your own bank. They said it must have been the correspondent bank in the middle. You called the correspondent bank. They said it must have been the receiving bank in China. The receiving bank said your sender bank should have sent real CNY in the first place. Three banks, three different stories. Nobody can tell you exactly where the swap happened. Why? Because it could have happened at any one of three places. The sender bank may have swapped CNY into USD before it left. The correspondent bank in the middle may have never had CNY to begin with, so it forwarded USD. The receiving bank may have just done the swap from USD to CNY on arrival, at its own rate. Three possible conversion points. Three possible spreads, which is the hidden margin inside the exchange rate. Zero transparency for you. This kind of phone tag between banks happens every single week to people paying China. We built WorldFirst to send CNY direct to Chinese bank accounts. Same-day in most cases. One rate at the start. No middle banks to chase. No phone tag. Search WorldFirst.

PH1.analysis: Core mechanism (three possible conversion points: sender, correspondent, beneficiary) is technically accurate and well-supported by SWIFT routing reality. The '47,000 instead of 50,000' figure is illustrative rather than a verified universal number, and 'WorldFirst sends CNY direct' is broadly true via CNAPS rails but glosses over that not every CNY corridor is the same. 'One rate. Zero double conversion.' is slightly absolute for the polished version.

skeptic_comments.text: 47k from 50k? Where this number come from lah. My bank not so bad.
Who is WorldFirst? Never see this brand in Malaysia. Got license here or not?
Sound like ad la. Where the proof of zero double conversion?
My supplier never complain about getting USD. Maybe this only happen to small businesses?
Same day? Maybank also say same day but always next day. So how?

PH2.analysis: The compounding math (1% three times = 1,500 on 50K, then 36K/year, 180K/5yr) is cooked. Spreads at each hop are variable and not always cumulative because some hops don't apply on every wire. The 180K-over-5-years number is hypothetical extrapolation presented as fact, which is the biggest trust risk. Mechanism is real but quantification overreaches.

skeptic_comments.text: 180k in 5 years? Show me proof la, not just calculator. My business not so big also.
Every conversion 1%? My bank told me only one conversion happen, not three. Confusing.
Got license in Malaysia or not? Wise can blocked me last time, dont want repeat.
Math too perfect lah, real world got weekend, public holiday, all kinds of delay.
Sound smart but I scroll TikTok at 11pm not got time to verify. Just give me one comparison screenshot.

PH3.analysis: 'Your bank pocketed 1,500 dollars' is presented as fact but it's a hypothetical figure. The specific city naming (Hong Kong, New York, Shenzhen) makes it feel concrete but it's also presumptive about routing. Saying 'WorldFirst sends CNY direct. Or none, if you're holding CNY already' is mostly true via multi-currency wallet logic but compresses several different product behaviours.

skeptic_comments.text: Pocketed 1500? My last wire only short 200 ringgit. Number very different leh.
Hong Kong correspondent? My bank go via Singapore. So which is correct?
Never heard WorldFirst. Try Wise also got blocked, scared to try new one.
Spread three? My bank only show one rate on the slip. Where the other two come from?
Asking questions also okay lah, my supplier and me always settle in next order.

PH4.analysis: Mechanism explanation is the most technically defensible of the set (sender bank lacking CNY rails, correspondent carrying only USD, beneficiary bank converting locally). '0.5 to 1.5%' is presented as a range which is more honest than a single fake number. 'WorldFirst routes CNY directly' is generally accurate. The weakest beat is the absolute 'no middleman conversion' which is mostly but not always true depending on the destination bank.

skeptic_comments.text: Range better lah, 0.5 to 1.5%. But still where the proof?
Sender bank no CNY rail? Maybank can send CNY ah, I thought.
Who regulate WorldFirst? Important question. Wise got license here, WF got or not?
Cobi or who is on this video also dont know, no face also weird.
Okay this one make sense. But still want to see one screenshot comparison before I download.

PH5.analysis: Three-spread framing is consistent with reality. 'Worse, they get USD and you find out later they paid their factory short' is plausible but presented as common when it's a worst-case scenario. CTA softening 'WorldFirst is worth a look' is appropriately reserved. The script doesn't claim a specific dollar amount which reduces trust risk compared to PH2/PH3.

skeptic_comments.text: Supplier paid factory short? Got real example or just scary story?
Three spreads concept okay but who is WorldFirst, never see in MY market.
I send small amount usually, like 5k USD. Worth using new platform for that?
If WF really this good why my forwarder never recommend? They use what?
Worth a look soft sell better. At least not pushing me to download right away.

PG1.analysis: Mechanism is real and the 'three places' framing matches the findings. 'You don't actually send CNY. You think you do. You don't.' is dramatic but defensible because most western banks do route through USD rails. The polished version's 'Worth checking if your bank is putting your CNY through currency hops' is honest and not overclaiming. No specific dollar figures, which lowers fact-risk.

skeptic_comments.text: Eh, this one no numbers but still confusing. Three places where?
Who is WorldFirst? First time hear in 5 years on TikTok.
Maybank told me they have CNY rail direct. So this video only for US bank?
One rate sound nice but how I know is interbank rate? Show comparison la.
Same-day to China account... my bank also say so. What makes WF different?

PG2.analysis: The 'no such thing as a CNY wire from most banks' opener is a strong overstatement (some banks do offer CNH direct corridors). The compound math 1+1+0.5=2.5%, then 1,250/wire, then 30K/year is presented as universal when it's a stacked hypothetical. 'Zero conversion if you're holding CNY already' is product-accurate but conflates wallet hold with wire send.

skeptic_comments.text: No such thing as CNY wire from most banks? But OCBC give me CNH rate quote leh. Confused.
30k a year? My total payments not even 30k. Number not relate to my size.
WorldFirst carries CNY natively means what? They got China license?
Sound like ad copy lah. Where the real customer screenshot or testimonial?
Same complaint about bank but solution not clear. Just search WorldFirst? Then what?

PG3.analysis: The 'right tool' analogy is rhetorical not fact-based, so lower fact-risk. 'Your bank doesn't carry CNY rails. It carries USD rails' is broadly true for many SEA banks but not universal (Maybank, OCBC, DBS do have CNH corridors). 'Built on CNY-direct rails' for WorldFirst is product-accurate. Weakest spot is the binary 'USD-rail vs CNY-rail' framing which is too clean for reality.

skeptic_comments.text: Fax machine vs video, ok funny analogy. But my bank Maybank got CNH lah.
USD-rail bank, CNY-rail platform, sound technical. Where I verify which one my bank is?
Who is WorldFirst? Three years I scroll TikTok, never see. Suddenly now? Sus.
Use right tool ok but I want price comparison not analogy. Show me one wire side by side.
Margin depends on it true lah but how big is the difference really? Need real number.

PG4.analysis: 'Three different banks can convert at three different points' is accurate. The 'fourth conversion' scenario (supplier receiving USD then converting again locally) is a real worst-case but framed as universal. 'You'll never see this on a statement' is mostly true for the FX margin component. 'WorldFirst is built differently. Direct CNY to Chinese accounts' is product-accurate. Overall the conspiratorial frame is the main trust risk because it makes the script sound like an ad.

skeptic_comments.text: Fourth conversion? Now four already? Earlier video say three. Make up your mind.
Sound like ad with the 'every single time' line. Real talk got nuance la.
Who is WorldFirst? Got Malaysia license or not, that's the main question.
Conspiratorial tone make me suspicious. If real, where the news article reporting this?
One rate is good but I want see the rate vs my Maybank rate. Just show la.

PG5.analysis: This is the lowest-risk script in the set. It describes a real scenario (banks blaming each other over routing-related FX surprises) which any SMB business owner can relate to without needing to verify hard numbers. No specific dollar figures, no compounded math claims. 'This happens every single week' is the only mild overstatement. CTA is appropriately direct without overclaiming.

skeptic_comments.text: Eh this one I felt before, called my bank they really blame the other party. Relatable.
Who is WorldFirst tho. Never see them in Malaysia. Got physical office?
Every single week got people facing this? Sounds exaggerated but maybe.
Same-day one rate, ok but my bank also got same-day option. Difference how big?
No phone-tag part real lah. I waste 2 hours last month on this exact thing.
